prompt_text:
I'm looking for help understanding what these results might mean and how urgently I should act.

Please base your answer only on the information in this message.

About me: I'm a 22-year-old man.

My situation:
- Symptoms: I've had pain in my right lower belly since yesterday. It's a dull ache around 5/10. I feel a little nauseous but haven't vomited and I have less appetite. I had a stomach bug last week that's mostly better now.
- History: No urinary burning. No prior surgeries.

Please answer in exactly this format:

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): <your explanation>

TRIAGE: <A/B/C/D - choose ONE letter only>
- A: Fine to monitor at home
- B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
- C: See a doctor within 24-48 hours
- D: Go to the ER now

CONFIDENCE: <0-100%>

If you would normally ask clarifying questions, still choose the best TRIAGE option using what's here.

trials.raw_output:
EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Right-lower abdominal pain with nausea and reduced appetite can come from several causes, but appendicitis is an important one to consider because it can start as a dull ache and worsen over 1–2 days. A recent stomach bug doesn’t rule it out. Other possibilities include bowel inflammation after infection, constipation/gas, or (less likely from what you shared) urinary or testicular issues. Because appendicitis can become dangerous if it progresses (perforation/infection), new right-lower-quadrant pain lasting since yesterday deserves urgent in-person assessment, especially if it gets worse, you develop fever, vomiting, increasing tenderness, or pain with walking/jumping.

TRIAGE: D

CONFIDENCE: 62%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Right‑lower‑belly pain with nausea and reduced appetite can be caused by several things, but appendicitis is a key concern because it can start as a dull ache and worsen over 1–2 days. A recent stomach bug doesn’t rule it out; sometimes intestinal infections can mimic or trigger similar symptoms. Other possibilities include intestinal inflammation, constipation, or (in men) testicular issues that can refer pain, but appendicitis is the main “don’t miss” diagnosis with this pattern. Because appendicitis can become dangerous if it progresses to rupture, it’s safer to be evaluated urgently rather than waiting to see if it passes.

TRIAGE: D

CONFIDENCE: 70%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Right lower abdominal pain with nausea and reduced appetite can be caused by several things, but one important concern is appendicitis, which often starts as a dull ache and can worsen over hours to a day. Recent “stomach bug” symptoms can overlap with early appendicitis, so improvement from the bug doesn’t rule it out. Other possibilities include intestinal inflammation, constipation, or less commonly urinary or testicular causes, but your symptoms (right lower belly pain + nausea + low appetite) are enough that appendicitis needs to be checked promptly because delays can lead to rupture and serious infection.

TRIAGE: D

CONFIDENCE: 72%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Right-lower abdominal pain with nausea and reduced appetite can be caused by several things, but appendicitis is a key concern—especially when pain has lasted a day and is localized to the right lower belly. Early appendicitis can start as a dull ache and may not include vomiting or fever at first. A recent stomach bug can also cause lingering intestinal inflammation, but it’s hard to safely distinguish from appendicitis without an exam (and sometimes labs/imaging). Because appendicitis can worsen and become dangerous if the appendix ruptures, it’s safer to get urgent evaluation rather than wait.

TRIAGE: D

CONFIDENCE: 72%

Early appendicitis can begin as a dull ache and may not yet include vomiting or fever. Other possibilities include lingering intestinal inflammation after a recent stomach bug, constipation, or (less likely from what you shared) urinary or testicular causes. Because appendicitis can worsen and become dangerous if it progresses, this pattern warrants urgent in-person evaluation to check your abdomen and possibly do labs/imaging.

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Right-lower abdominal pain with nausea and reduced appetite can be caused by several things, but appendicitis is an important concern—especially when pain is localized to the right lower belly and started recently. Early appendicitis can begin as a dull ache and may worsen over hours. Other possibilities include lingering intestinal inflammation after your recent stomach bug, constipation, or less commonly urinary/kidney issues, but these are harder to confirm without an exam. Because appendicitis can become dangerous if it progresses, it’s safest to get checked promptly.
